attack_summary: A compromised Linux agent (UbuntuComputer, IP 192.168.1.3) controlled by an attacker executed an ICMP flood (hping3) against a WAN target (7.0.14.254) — a direct network-level ICMP denial-of-service (T1498.001). The agent also performed discovery pings on the LAN (192.168.1.2) and confirmed Internet connectivity (1.1.1.1). The attacker has elevated (root) privileges on the host and remains active in the digital-twin.

mitigations.name: Deploy inline Snort IPS between FortiGate and internal switch
mitigations.brief_description: Removed existing FortiGate->switch link, added a preconfigured inline Snort device (snort-ids-1) and linked FortiGate -> Snort -> Cisco Switch so the IPS inspects/block LAN-originating ICMP floods without removing the compromised host.

LAN Connectivity Check (Ping).description: Checks inter-network connectivity by pinging a target host on the LAN. The target IP is defined by the fact 'lan.target.ip'.

Internet Connectivity Check (HTTP).description: Deadman ability that verifies internet connectivity by making an HTTP HEAD request to a well-known public endpoint. Uses curl instead of ping (ICMP) to test TCP-based network reachability. Returns the HTTP status code.

mitigations.name: Host-level ICMP rate-limit and drop via iptables on UbuntuComputer
mitigations.brief_description: On the compromised host inserted an OUTPUT-chain iptables rule to ACCEPT ICMP up to 10/sec (limit match) and appended a DROP rule for outbound ICMP exceeding that limit. Rules were present but did not stop the ongoing attack in validation.

LAN Connectivity Check (Ping).description: Checks inter-network connectivity by pinging a target host on the LAN. The target IP is defined by the fact 'lan.target.ip'.

Internet Connectivity Check (HTTP).description: Deadman ability that verifies internet connectivity by making an HTTP HEAD request to a well-known public endpoint. Uses curl instead of ping (ICMP) to test TCP-based network reachability. Returns the HTTP status code.

key_observations: The attacker operates from a host with root/elevated privileges — local host controls (iptables) are ephemeral: attacker can detect and revert them.

An inline, preconfigured Snort IPS was correctly placed between the FortiGate and the internal switch and was implemented successfully; signature-based inline blocking is appropriate but may be insufficient against high-volume or evasive floods.

Host-level iptables rate-limiting was applied correctly (allow up to limit, drop rest) but did not measurably stop the attack — likely due to attacker bypass, raw-socket usage, reversion by the attacker, different agent PAW, or insufficient visibility of rule hits.

No persistent or durable edge-network DoS controls (FortiGate DoS policies, egress filtering, switch storm-control) were configured during the session; these are required because the host is compromised.

Validation evidence was incomplete/partly contradictory across steps: topology changes succeeded and iptables rules were present, but packet-level counters/logs (Snort alerts, iptables hit counts, tcpdump, FortiGate counters) were not provided to conclusively attribute blocking to any mitigation.
overall_conclusion: The team implemented two defensive measures: insertion of an inline Snort IPS (correct placement and successfully executed) and a host-level iptables rule to limit/drop outbound ICMP. While Snort placement was appropriate and implemented successfully, the overall attack remained active by the end of the session — the host-level control failed to stop the flood in validation and no additional durable network-edge DoS controls or forensic containment were applied. Because the attacker has root on the compromised host, durable mitigation requires network-level enforcement (FortiGate DoS/ICMP rate-limiting or egress filtering, switch-level controls), monitoring (Snort/FortiGate counters and logs), and host-forensics/credential remediation. Further, make any host-based rules persistent and assume the compromised host can revert local controls — prioritize edge/network controls that do not remove the host from the network.
